1: Ps 150: The Grand Doxology — Conclusion of the Psalter. Ps 150 is the Psalter's conclusion: a pure, undiluted doxology that answers the Psalter's five lament books with a final, sustained, instrument-by-instrument praise. There is no petition, no lament, no narrative — only the summons to praise, the location of praise, the reason for praise, and the means of praise. The number of imperative verbs in vv1-5 is debated but significant: 10-13 'praises' summons, corresponding perhaps to the Ten Commandments or the Decalogue's call to covenant relationship. 'Praise the LORD! Praise God in his sanctuary; praise him in his mighty heavens!' — 'his sanctuary' (miqdosho) and 'his mighty heavens' (raqia uzzo) — the two locations of praise: the earthly temple (the specific locus of Israel's worship) and the heavenly firmament (the cosmic arena). The earthly and heavenly dimensions of worship are both valid.
2: 'Praise him for his mighty deeds; praise him according to his excellent greatness!' — the two grounds for praise: 'his mighty deeds' (gevurotav) — the acts of divine power in history (Exodus, Conquest, Return from exile); 'his excellent greatness' (kegodol tuvo) — his intrinsic greatness, independent of any specific act. The acts reveal the character; the character grounds the acts. Both are proper grounds for praise: what YHWH has done (salvation history) and who YHWH is (divine nature).
3: 'Praise him with trumpet sound; praise him with lute and harp!' — the instrument catalogue begins. 'Trumpet' (shofar) — the ram's horn, the instrument of covenant assembly (Lev 23:24; Num 10:10), of the Jubilee proclamation (Lev 25:9), and of Sinai theophany (Exod 19:16). 'Lute' (nevel) and 'harp' (kinnor) — the stringed instruments of Levitical praise (1 Chr 25:1-7). The trumpet's commanding sound is joined by the lyrical strings: proclamation and melody together.
4: 'Praise him with tambourine and dance; praise him with strings and pipe!' — 'tambourine and dance' (toph umachol) — the percussion and physical movement of embodied worship (cf. Ps 149:3; Miriam's dance, Exod 15:20; David's dance, 2 Sam 6:14). 'Strings and pipe' (minim veuggav) — further stringed instruments and the wind instrument (uggav, possibly a flute or pan-pipe). The full range of instrumental timbre: percussion, strings, wind, and bodily movement — worship employing every physical faculty.
5: 'Praise him with sounding cymbals; praise him with loud clashing cymbals!' — 'sounding cymbals' (tziltzalei shama) and 'loud clashing cymbals' (tziltzalei teru'ah) — two varieties of cymbal: one ringing, one crashing. The cymbals were part of the Levitical orchestra (1 Chr 13:8; 15:16,19,28; 16:5). The final instrument is the loudest, most percussive: the Psalter's doxology crescendos rather than fades. Paul's famous 'noisy gong or clanging cymbal' (1 Cor 13:1) uses this verse's vocabulary to describe love-less speech — the cymbal's noise without love's content is empty, but in Ps 150 the cymbals have their proper context.
6: 'Let everything that has breath praise the LORD! Praise the LORD!' — the Psalter's final word: 'everything that has breath' (kol haneshama tehalel Yah). The neshamah (breath) — the breath of life that YHWH breathed into Adam (Gen 2:7: 'he breathed into his nostrils the breath of life'). Every creature possessing the breath of God is called to return praise to God. The Psalter that began with 'Blessed is the man' (Ps 1:1) and moved through 150 psalms of lament, praise, wisdom, and doxology ends at the universal: every breathing creature is summoned to praise. The final 'Hallelujah!' is simultaneously the end of the Psalter and an opening — the praise that cannot be concluded.
